title: Oxygen Loss at Meridian Station
briefing: At 03:00 station time, the oxygen scrubber in Module C failed without warning, tripping an emergency alarm across the Meridian Mars outpost. No one was hurt -- the crew reached the safe module in time -- but the timing is strange: an Earth Oversight Council audit team is due to arrive within the week.

suspects.name: Aiko Reyes
suspects.persona: Composed and protocol-driven, carries the weight of crew safety visibly. Answers precisely, like she's already anticipating an inquiry board.

timeline.text: I initiated the emergency lockdown the moment the alarm tripped and had everyone into the safe module within four minutes.

motive.text: I have nothing to gain from an equipment failure. An incident like this triggers a full inquiry that could end my command.

relationships.text: I approved Dr. Voss's data archive access requests the same as anyone else's, routine sign-offs. Nothing about them stood out to me.

alibi.text: I was in the command module the entire time, running the response.

evidenceReactions.text: ...I deferred that safety round this week and meant to hand it to Dmitri directly. The message never went through properly. That's on me, not him.

backgroundCheck.text: Command record spotless. Twelve years of service without a prior incident.

initialLie.text: I personally verified the Module C scrubber system during this week's safety rounds, it was in good working order.

suspects.name: Dmitri Kessler
suspects.persona: Technical and gruff, proud of the station's redundant systems, visibly defensive whenever equipment reliability comes into question.

timeline.text: The scrubber malfunction tripped at oh-three-hundred. I had the backup system online within ninety seconds.

motive.text: This station's systems are my responsibility and my reputation. A failure like this makes me look bad, it doesn't help me.

relationships.text: I work with everyone on this crew daily. Nothing between me and Dr. Voss beyond routine equipment requests.

alibi.text: I was asleep in my quarters until the alarm woke me, same as most of the crew.

evidenceReactions.text: ...There was an amber flag on the backup sensor, yes. It was low priority, queued behind higher-severity tickets. In hindsight I should have escalated it.

backgroundCheck.text: Certified engineer, six years on-station, no disciplinary record.

initialLie.text: I ran a full diagnostic on every life support system just two days ago, everything was flagged green across the board.

suspects.name: Sofia Lindqvist
suspects.persona: Calm under pressure, clinical in her phrasing, notices physiological and behavioral details others miss.

timeline.text: I treated two crew members for minor smoke inhalation afterward. Nothing life-threatening.

motive.text: My job is keeping this crew alive. An oxygen incident is the last thing I'd want.

relationships.text: I see everyone professionally at some point, physicals, checkups. Nothing outside of that with Dr. Voss specifically.

alibi.text: I was in the medical bay finishing charting when the alarm went off.

evidenceReactions.text: ...Fine, I stepped into the bunk next door for about forty minutes. It's permitted during a quiet shift. I just didn't think it was relevant to mention.

backgroundCheck.text: Licensed and current. No irregularities in her service record.

initialLie.text: I was awake and alert in the med bay the entire night, I don't miss things on my shift.

suspects.name: Jonas Okafor
suspects.persona: Chatty and restless, openly counting down the days until rotation home, a little too eager to speculate about what happened.

timeline.text: I was pulling a late shift monitoring the Earth-relay window when the alarm tripped.

motive.text: I've got four months left on this rotation and every reason to want a quiet, uneventful tour. This is the opposite of what I want.

relationships.text: I talk to everyone on this crew, it's the job. Nothing specific with Dr. Voss beyond routine chatter.

alibi.text: I was at the comms station the whole time. The relay logs are timestamped to the second, you can check.

evidenceReactions.text: ...Okay, I did step out for about five minutes to grab a ration pack near Module C. It's a two-minute walk from comms. I didn't think it needed mentioning.

backgroundCheck.text: No irregularities. Standard performance reviews, on track for rotation home.

initialLie.text: I hadn't left the comms station for even a minute all shift.

suspects.name: Petra Voss
suspects.persona: Precise and controlled, gives measured, professional answers -- and grows noticeably more clipped and defensive the closer the conversation gets to her research timeline or data.

timeline.text: I was finalizing data backups ahead of the audit team's arrival next week. That's normal pre-audit preparation.

motive.text: The station's funding depends on this research program showing results. Everyone here has a stake in it succeeding, not just me.

relationships.text: I coordinate with the whole crew on data logistics. Nothing outside of the professional.

alibi.text: I was alone in the archive room finishing backups when the alarm went off. I made it to the safe module with everyone else.

evidenceReactions.text: ...I did access that terminal, briefly. I was checking humidity levels for a sample storage concern, nothing to do with the scrubber itself. The timing is a coincidence.

backgroundCheck.text: Data revision log flagged: multiple research datasets show late-cycle edits inconsistent with original sample timestamps, submitted in the weeks immediately preceding a scheduled research audit.

initialLie.text: I never touched the environmental controls, that's not part of my role at all.

evidence.label: Maintenance scheduling log
evidence.description: This week's Module C safety round was deferred by Commander Reyes and handed off verbally to the engineering team, but the handoff was never logged or confirmed.

evidence.label: Systems diagnostic log
evidence.description: A diagnostic run two days prior flagged Module C's backup scrubber redundancy sensor amber -- a low-priority warning queued for follow-up that had not yet occurred.

evidence.label: Medical bay shift log
evidence.description: The medical bay's occupancy log shows Dr. Lindqvist logged roughly forty minutes resting in the adjoining bunk during the night shift, ending shortly before the alarm.

evidence.label: Corridor badge access log
evidence.description: A badge scan shows Jonas Okafor briefly left the comms station and accessed the corridor near Module C roughly twenty minutes before the incident.

evidence.label: Environmental control terminal log
evidence.description: The archive wing's environmental control terminal logged a manual override command sent to the Module C scrubber system at 02:58, ninety seconds before the malfunction alarm tripped.

timeline.text: Night shift begins; most of the crew retires to quarters except scheduled watch personnel.
Petra Voss says she was in the archive room finalizing pre-audit data backups.
The archive wing's environmental control terminal logs a manual override command sent to the Module C scrubber system.
The Module C oxygen scrubber malfunctions; an emergency alarm trips station-wide.
Aiko Reyes says she initiated the emergency lockdown within moments of the alarm.
Dmitri Kessler says he had the backup life-support system online within ninety seconds.
All crew are accounted for and sealed in the station's safe module within four minutes of the alarm.
Sofia Lindqvist says she was awake and alert in the med bay the entire night.
Jonas Okafor says he hadn't left the comms station for even a minute all shift.
Backup scrubbers stabilize oxygen levels; the immediate emergency passes without injury.

solution.narrative:
The oxygen scrubber malfunction in Module C was not an accident. Dr. Petra Voss engineered it deliberately, using her terminal access to the environmental control systems to trigger a controlled, survivable failure -- close enough to force an emergency response, but never actually life-threatening, since she made sure everyone would reach the safe module in time.

Her real problem wasn't the station's air. It was the Earth Oversight Council's surprise research audit, scheduled to arrive within the week. Months of atmospheric and soil sample data she'd submitted under funding pressure had been quietly backfilled and adjusted to match deadlines she'd missed in the field -- fabrications an experienced auditor would catch in an afternoon. An emergency serious enough to justify wiping the archive's physical backups "for safety" would destroy the evidence before anyone thought to look for it.

She was in the archive room exactly when she said she was, doing exactly what she said she was doing -- backups. She simply didn't mention that the emergency she was backing up from was one she'd caused herself, ninety seconds earlier, from the same terminal.
